statusLine: EU fuel security is under strain with 4 of 27 countries in critical status across all three fuel types, while oil prices have surged 30.4% since February amid Middle East supply disruptions.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel security faces mounting pressure as oil prices continue their sharp ascent, with Brent crude reaching $94.55 per barrel—a 30.4% increase since late February when Middle East tensions escalated. While the EU maintains an average of 113 days of petrol reserves and 102.1 days of diesel reserves, both above the mandatory 90-day minimum, significant disparities exist across member states.

Romania presents the most concerning situation with critical status across all fuel types: just 40.4 days of petrol, 30.2 days of diesel, and 76.4 days of jet fuel reserves. Poland and Luxembourg also face critical shortages in all three categories, while Italy has critical petrol and diesel reserves despite adequate jet fuel stocks. These vulnerabilities are particularly troubling given the strategic reserve releases several countries have already implemented to manage supply disruptions.

Jet fuel emerges as the most vulnerable fuel type, with 17 of 27 countries below the 90-day threshold and an EU average of just 96.4 days. This weakness spans major economies including Germany (64.7 days) and France (67.5 days), highlighting aviation fuel as a key pressure point. The sustained oil price rally is simultaneously driving up costs for consumers, with pump prices averaging €1.851 per liter for petrol and €1.87 per liter for diesel across the EU.
keyPoints: 4 of 27 EU countries face critical status across all three fuel types, with Romania showing the most severe shortages at 40.4 days petrol and 30.2 days diesel
Jet fuel represents the weakest link with 17 of 27 countries below 90-day reserves, including major economies like Germany and France
Oil prices have surged 30.4% since February's Middle East escalation, reaching $94.55 per barrel and pressuring both supply security and consumer costs
Despite EU-wide averages above mandatory minimums, strategic reserve releases by several countries indicate ongoing supply management challenges
Price disparities are significant, with Danish petrol at €2.50 per liter nearly double Malta's €1.34 rate
